message:
chore: update issue tracker bead statuses (concurrent agent progress)

Minor status transitions in .beads/issues.jsonl from ongoing parallel
agent work on franken_node beads.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -219,7 +219,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-1now.5.3","title":"Add repo-level semantic-boundary proof harness with E2E cases and detailed logs","description":"Why this exists:
The semantic-boundary policy will not stay trustworthy if it only exists as a matrix plus low-level checks. The repo needs a scripted proof lane that demonstrates both forbidden and allowed cases with detailed, reusable diagnostics.

What this task must do:
Add a repository-level validation harness for the semantic-boundary policy, with deterministic positive and negative cases.

The harness should exercise at minimum:
- a forbidden duplicate/fork case that must fail,
- an undocumented boundary crossing case that must fail,
- at least one explicitly allowed local-model/spec case that must pass,
- at least one canonical-alignment case that must pass or route to the documented upstream rule.

Required outputs:
- at least one deterministic script under `tests/e2e/` or a tightly justified equivalent repo-level harness,
- machine-readable summary output,
- human-readable detailed logs/artifacts,
- stable reason codes / rule ids that map directly back to the semantic-boundary policy.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The semantic-boundary cluster has deterministic repository-level validation coverage for both forbidden and allowed cases.
2. The harness emits both machine-readable summaries and human-readable detailed logs/artifacts.
3. Failures identify the violated boundary rule with stable reason codes / rule ids and enough context for contributors to fix the issue without policy archaeology.
4. Machine-readable outputs carry stable case identifiers, schema/version markers, and artifact locations that map back to the semantic-boundary matrix and anti-drift rule ids.
5. The harness is reusable by the closeout gate and future regressions, not a one-off proof script.
6. Any cargo-heavy or broad validation required by this lane is executed through `rch`.","notes":"Treat this like a policy E2E lane, not a documentation demo. The point is to prove that the boundary policy can catch real mistakes and preserve intentionally local surfaces without confusing contributors.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:39:56.252355123Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T22:47:21.028884411Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","boundaries","e2e","rch","self-contained","test-obligations","testing","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:39:56.252355123Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:39:56.683187486Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.6","title":"Deferred trigger: adopt native Asupersync request-region boundaries when a real async server lands","description":"Why this exists:
The audit deliberately rejected an immediate native-Asupersync migration of the control-plane service skeleton because `crates/franken-node/src/api/service.rs` is still a skeleton/catalog layer rather than a live async HTTP or gRPC server boundary.

What this deferred task must preserve for future self:
If `franken_node` later grows a real async network boundary, that is the moment to revisit native Asupersync request-region/capability-narrowing patterns.

Future trigger conditions:
- real socket accept loop or gRPC call boundary is introduced in this crate,
- request cancellation and per-request lifecycle become real runtime concerns,
- middleware begins owning spawn/time/IO authority rather than just metadata,
- the crate needs deterministic request shutdown/drain semantics.

When the trigger fires, the future implementation should evaluate:
- request-region ownership,
- narrowed request `Cx` capability exposure,
- explicit lifecycle/Outcome mapping at transport boundaries,
- whether the crate should own an `AppSpec`-like supervised service topology.

Why this bead exists now:
It prevents future contributors from forgetting that the right answer for HTTP/gRPC boundaries may eventually be native Asupersync — while also making clear that today is not that day.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The deferred bead captures the exact trigger conditions that would justify revisiting native Asupersync request/call boundary patterns in `franken_node`.
2. The bead makes it explicit that the current service skeleton is not yet sufficient justification for a migration.
3. Future contributors can pick this bead up without needing to rediscover why the work was deferred, what proof obligations wake it up, and what evidence shape the eventual cluster must produce.","notes":"This bead should remain deferred until the crate owns a real async server boundary. Treat premature activation as a design smell.

When this bead is eventually activated, the very first execution move should be a fresh design + verification cluster that includes:
- request-boundary ownership/lifecycle design,
- deterministic unit tests for boundary semantics,
- transport-level end-to-end scripts,
- structured lifecycle logging/artifacts with stable schema/version markers,
- baseline comparison against predecessor transport behavior where relevant,
- operator/contributor guidance for any exposed tuning or backpressure budgets,
- `rch`-offloaded heavy cargo validation.","status":"deferred","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.260873625Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:29:50.832026969Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","deferred-trigger","grpc","http","runtime","self-contained"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.260873625Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:39:57.353548031Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.7","title":"Deferred trigger: promote singleton/mutex state owners only when topology justifies actor-style ownership","description":"Why this exists:
The audit found several singleton/mutex-backed or shared-owner surfaces in `franken_node` (for example shared fleet control manager and shared evidence ledger wrappers). Those do not automatically deserve actor/GenServer migration today, but some of them might if the concurrency topology becomes richer.

What this deferred task must preserve for future self:
Record the trigger conditions for promoting singleton/mutex-backed state owners into explicit actor/GenServer/service-lifecycle patterns.

Candidate surfaces to reevaluate later include:
- `api/fleet_quarantine.rs` shared fleet control manager,
- `observability/evidence_ledger.rs` shared ledger wrapper,
- any state owner that later gains reply obligations, named-worker semantics, restart domains, or meaningful concurrent mutation pressure.

Future trigger conditions:
- one natural owner is hidden behind broad shared mutable state,
- callers begin building ad hoc request/reply protocols around channels or locks,
- restart policy or failure isolation becomes important,
- lifecycle/ownership bugs become hard to reason about,
- contention or hidden coordination costs become measurable.

Why this bead exists now:
It gives future contributors a principled promotion rule instead of a style-war rule.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The deferred bead names the main singleton/shared-owner surfaces worth reevaluating later.
2. The bead captures the trigger conditions under which actor/GenServer/service-lifecycle promotion becomes justified.
3. The bead makes clear that today’s decision is “defer until topology demands it,” not “never improve these surfaces,” and preserves the proof/logging expectations the eventual activation must satisfy.","notes":"Do not activate this bead just because a `Mutex` exists. Activate it when ownership, reply protocol, failure domain, restart semantics, or measurable coordination cost become the real problem.

When this bead is eventually activated, start with a fresh design + proof cluster that includes:
- actor/service ownership contract,
- deterministic unit tests for request/reply and restart semantics,
- end-to-end lifecycle/failure scripts where operator-visible behavior changes,
- structured logging / artifact capture with stable schema/version markers,
- baseline comparison against predecessor behavior where relevant,
- operator/contributor guidance for any exposed tuning or coordination budgets,
- `rch`-offloaded heavy cargo validation.","status":"deferred","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.680337102Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:29:51.049740055Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["actors","architecture","asupersync","deferred-trigger","runtime","self-contained"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.7","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.680337102Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.7","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:39:57.596270348Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-1now.8","title":"Selective Asupersync verification gate: comprehensive unit+e2e+logging + rch evidence","description":"Why this exists:
The selective Asupersync plan should not close on architecture prose alone. The repo needs one explicit closeout gate that proves the immediate work actually delivered the intended robustness/diagnostic improvements without regressions.

What this task must do:
Collect and run the proof obligations for the immediate selective-Asupersync work.

This closeout gate should include:
- the Tokio/runtime guardrail proof harness,
- telemetry unit/integration regression coverage,
- telemetry end-to-end orchestration scripts and artifact review,
- semantic-boundary anti-drift safeguards,
- offloaded `rch` validation for heavy cargo check/clippy/test surfaces,
- machine-readable and human-readable evidence artifacts summarizing results.

Why this matters:
This is what lets the epic close with evidence instead of “the plan looked good and the code compiled once.”","acceptance_criteria":"1. Heavy validation for the selective-Asupersync work is run through `rch`, including the relevant cargo check/clippy/test surfaces for the touched implementation.
2. The Tokio/runtime guardrail checker plus its negative and allowed-case fixtures pass with clear failure-mode evidence.
3. Telemetry unit/integration tests, telemetry end-to-end orchestration scripts, telemetry performance/budget characterization, and telemetry operator-reference outputs all pass or land with structured lifecycle / failure logs preserved.
4. Semantic-boundary anti-drift safeguards and the repo-level semantic-boundary proof harness both pass and leave reusable evidence.
5. The epic produces a compact evidence pack (machine-readable plus human-readable) with stable schema/version markers, baseline-vs-after comparison where relevant, tuning/threshold context where exposed, and enough cross-links to the operator reference and semantic matrix that future contributors can inspect it without rerunning the whole investigation from scratch.","notes":"This is the closeout gate for the immediate selective-Asupersync work, not a general program-wide gate. Prefer reusable artifact names and stable log schemas so later regressions can compare against them directly.

The evidence pack should include behavioral proof, event-accounting proof, performance-envelope proof, baseline-comparison context, and operator-facing interpretation for the telemetry cluster, plus policy-proof artifacts for the semantic-boundary cluster, so users/operators are not forced to choose between correctness confidence and runtime practicality.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:49.694784258Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:29:51.273384606Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","e2e","rch","self-contained","test-obligations","testing","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:49.694784258Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:33:28.640830761Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:33:58.688223616Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:37:47.313678245Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:42:39.022539074Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:39:58.280206358Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1o4v","title":"[10.18] Implement proof-verification gate API for control-plane trust decisions.","description":"## Why This Exists

Section 10.18 implements the Verifiable Execution Fabric (VEF) — Enhancement Map 9L — delivering proof-carrying runtime compliance. This is a Track C (Trust-Native Ecosystem Layer) critical gate that makes franken_node's trust decisions depend on cryptographic verification rather than trust-by-assertion.

This bead implements the proof-verification gate API — the component that control-plane trust decisions invoke to validate proofs before authorizing high-risk actions or trust transitions. The proof-generation service (bd-1u8m) produces proofs; this gate validates them. It is the trust boundary between "a proof exists" and "the proof is valid and binds to the correct policy and receipt window."

This gate is security-critical: it must be fail-closed (invalid or missing proofs default to denial), must validate the full binding chain (proof -> receipt-window commitment -> policy hash), and must produce stable verdict classes that downstream components (bd-8qlj for control transitions, bd-3pds for verifier SDK) can reason about deterministically.

## What This Must Do

1. Implement a verification gate API that accepts a proof envelope (from bd-1u8m) and validates it against the receipt-window commitment and policy hash binding.
2. Validate the full binding chain: proof envelope -> proof validity -> receipt-window commitment match -> policy hash match.
3. Return stable, classified verdict types: `VALID`, `INVALID_PROOF`, `MISSING_PROOF`, `STALE_POLICY`, `COMMITMENT_MISMATCH`, `VERIFICATION_ERROR`.
4. Enforce fail-closed semantics: any verification failure or missing input defaults to denial with a specific verdict class.
5. Support multiple proof backends transparently — the gate verifies proofs regardless of which backend generated them, using backend-specific verification parameters from the proof envelope.
6. Produce auditable verification records: every gate invocation (pass or fail) emits a signed, replayable verification record.
7. Expose a synchronous API suitable for hot-path integration in control-plane trust decisions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Verification gate validates proof, receipt-window commitment, and policy hash binding; invalid/missing proofs return stable fail-closed verdict classes.
- All verdict classes are stable, enumerated, and documented with remediation guidance.
- Fail-closed behavior: missing proof -> `MISSING_PROOF` denial; malformed proof -> `INVALID_PROOF` denial; expired policy binding -> `STALE_POLICY` denial.
- Multi-backend support: proofs from different backends validate correctly through the same gate API.
- Verification records are signed and contain enough context for independent replay.
- Gate latency is bounded and suitable for synchronous control-plane integration.
- No silent pass-through: the gate never returns an affirmative verdict without completing full validation.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests for each verdict class: valid proof, invalid proof, missing proof, stale policy, commitment mismatch, verification error.
- Fail-closed tests: null inputs, partial inputs, corrupted proof bytes — all must return denial verdicts.
- Multi-backend tests: validate proofs from the reference backend and any additional backends through the same gate.
- Binding chain tests: modify the receipt-window commitment after proof generation — must detect mismatch; modify policy hash — must detect stale policy.
- Replay tests: verification records contain sufficient context to re-execute the verification and reach the same verdict.
- Latency benchmark: measure gate verification time under load, confirm it stays within control-plane hot-path budget.
- Structured logging: `VEF-VERIFY-001` (verification requested), `VEF-VERIFY-002` (verdict issued), `VEF-VERIFY-ERR-*` (per verdict class).
- Trace correlation IDs linking verification requests to proof job IDs, receipt window ranges, and originating action contexts.

## Expected Artifacts

- `src/trust/vef_verification_gate.rs` — proof-verification gate API implementation.
- `tests/security/vef_verification_gate.rs` — security-focused tests for all verdict classes and fail-closed behavior.
- `artifacts/10.18/vef_verification_gate_report.json` — verification gate test report with verdict class coverage.
- `artifacts/section_10_18/bd-1o4v/verification_evidence.json` — machine-readable CI/release gate evidence.
- `artifacts/section_10_18/bd-1o4v/verification_summary.md` — human-readable summary linking intent to measured outcomes.

## Dependencies

- bd-1u8m (blocks) — Proof-generation service interface: produces the proof envelopes that this gate validates.

Dependents: bd-8qlj (VEF integration into control transitions), bd-3pds (verifier SDK integration), bd-2hjg (section gate), bd-32p (plan-level tracker).","acceptance_criteria":"- Verification gate validates proof, receipt-window commitment, and policy hash binding; invalid/missing proofs return stable fail-closed verdict classes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:04.625740872Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T07:05:58.155167786Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T07:05:58.155130326Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-18","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1o4v","depends_on_id":"bd-1u8m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:05:51.460063573Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1ofy","title":"NaN fail-open in anti_amplification validate_policy bypasses ratio check","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-07T11:50:11.226584066Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-07T11:50:25.841977960Z","closed_at":"2026-03-07T11:50:25.841860601Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1oju","title":"[10.4 bug] Bind trust cards and certification to verified evidence, not asserted trust inputs","description":"Why this exists:
The deeper 10.4 failure is downstream, not just at registry admission. `crates/franken-node/src/supply_chain/trust_card.rs` defines `TrustCardInput` as a bag of caller-supplied trust facts and then signs whatever it is given, while `crates/franken-node/src/supply_chain/certification.rs` evaluates `CertificationInput` from caller-supplied provenance/reputation/evidence fields instead of deriving them from verified upstream state. That means the project can mint polished, signed-looking trust cards and policy certification decisions from unverified assertions.

What this bead must do:
Bind trust-card materialization and certification to verified evidence handles, not free-form caller assertions. The design target is a typed trust pipeline: registry-admitted artifacts produce immutable verified evidence records; trust cards and certification consume those records by reference; every rendered trust claim is traceable back to canonical manifest/provenance/reputation/revocation evidence. Compile the abstruse parts into concrete artifacts: evidence IDs, append-only derivation receipts, minimal counterexample witnesses for disputed trust decisions, and deterministic replay bundles that let another verifier reconstruct why a card or certification level existed. Use the mathematically ambitious ideas only where they buy real leverage: Galois-style monotone trust derivations, lattice-safe demotion rules, and fraud-proof style witnesses for downstream trust disagreements.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Trust cards can no longer be created or mutated from raw caller-supplied provenance/reputation/certification claims; they must be derived from verified upstream evidence records or fail closed.
2. Certification evaluation consumes verified provenance/reputation/evidence references, not ad hoc booleans and free-form asserted levels.
3. Every trust card and certification result carries derivation metadata that links back to specific verified manifest/provenance/revocation/reputation inputs and survives deterministic replay.
4. Demotion and quarantine flows are monotone: once upstream evidence degrades or is revoked, downstream trust cards/certification cannot silently remain at the old level.
5. `bd-2yh`, `bd-273`, and `bd-y4g` share one canonical trust-derivation kernel instead of each accepting local asserted trust inputs.
6. Adversarial fixtures cover caller-injected fake provenance summaries, forged reputation jumps, stale certification evidence, conflicting upstream inputs, and trust-card mutation attempts that bypass verified evidence binding.
7. Add a checker that fails if replacement-critical trust surfaces reintroduce input structs or code paths that let callers inject final trust claims without verified evidence handles.
8. Deliver unit tests, integration/E2E trust-materialization fixtures, structured logs, and machine-readable derivation artifacts proving that downstream trust objects are grounded in verified state.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Trust-card and certification APIs consume verified evidence handles only; callers cannot supply final trust facts, provenance summaries, or certification levels directly on any replacement-critical path.
2. Downstream trust behavior is user-explainable: every trust card or certification result carries a derivation tree, downgrade and quarantine reasons, and stable reason codes suitable for UI, CLI, and incident bundles.
3. Unit tests cover handle typing, monotone demotion rules, revocation propagation, conflicting upstream evidence resolution, stale evidence rejection, and regression checks against asserted-input shortcuts.
4. Integration tests exercise registry admit -> trust-card materialization -> certification -> revocation -> downgrade -> quarantine -> recovery, proving downstream state changes track upstream evidence exactly.
5. At least one deterministic operator E2E shell script under tests/e2e/ reproduces trust-card issuance, certification changes, revocation-driven demotion, and quarantine behavior with detailed logs and replayable fixtures.
6. Structured logs expose stable TRUST_DERIVATION_* and CERTIFICATION_DECISION_* event families with required fields: trace_id, evidence_ids, subject_id, previous_level, new_level, reason_code, and replay_bundle_id.
7. Evidence artifacts under artifacts/replacement_gap/bd-1oju/ include machine-readable derivation receipts, human-readable trust summaries, adversarial fixture indexes, and rch offload build IDs for the verification run.
8. Completion requires a static regression checker that fails if replacement-critical trust surfaces reintroduce free-form asserted-input structs or bypass verified evidence handles.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T00:19:43.346293372Z","created_by":"RoseMountain","updated_at":"2026-03-09T06:07:05.867335162Z","closed_at":"2026-03-09T06:07:05.867202615Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-optimization","extreme-optimization","gap-closure","replacement-critical","section-10-0","section-10-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1oju","depends_on_id":"bd-3hdn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T00:20:36.815255369Z","created_by":"RoseMountain","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1z5a.6","title":"bd-1z5a support: replace synthetic verifier_result signatures in connector verifier_sdk","description":"Narrow support shard under bd-1z5a.  {"id":"bd-1z5a.7","title":"bd-1z5a support: harden replay capsule binding fields and trace commitments","description":"Audit on 2026-03-10 found that bd-1z5a remains open because verifier-economy replay capsule integrity is still under-bound. Current ReplayCapsule in crates/franken-node/src/verifier_economy/mod.rs only carries capsule_id, attestation_id, input_state_hash, execution_trace_hash, output_state_hash, expected_result_hash, and integrity_hash. Current compute_capsule_integrity_hash in crates/franken-node/src/connector/verifier_sdk.rs hashes only those same six fields. That leaves parent acceptance criteria 2 and 3 unmet: no explicit verifier identity, claim/version metadata, freshness envelope or issuance timestamp, and no chunked/Merkleized trace commitment structure for minimal fraud-proof validation. This support shard should harden the replay-capsule binding surface, preferably by aligning the verifier-economy shape with the richer signed capsule concepts already present in connector/universal_verifier_sdk.rs and/or sdk/replay_capsule.rs, while keeping the change isolated to the replay-capsule verification path and adding focused regression coverage.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WindyDesert","created_at":"2026-03-10T05:15:17.112537956Z","created_by":"WindyDesert","updated_at":"2026-03-10T05:33:59.247081880Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T05:33:59.247053407Z","close_reason":"Implemented replay-capsule binding hardening: schema version, verifier identity, claim metadata hash, freshness window, chunked trace commitments, updated integrity hash binding, and focused regression coverage. Local verifier checkers/tests pass; workspace rch cargo validation is currently blocked by unrelated ops/tokio_drift_checker.rs and ops/telemetry_bridge.rs errors.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1z5a.7","depends_on_id":"bd-1z5a","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-10T05:15:17.112537956Z","created_by":"WindyDesert","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1z9s","title":"[10.13] Implement transparency-log inclusion proof checks in install/update pipelines.","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 10.13 — FCP Deep-Mined Expansion Execution Track (9I)

Why This Exists:
Deep connector/provider contract track: lifecycle FSMs, conformance harnesses, fencing, sandboxing, revocation freshness, and protocol hardening.

Task Objective:
Implement transparency-log inclusion proof checks in install/update pipelines.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Install/update fails if required inclusion proof is missing/invalid; log roots are pinned per policy; verification path is replayable.

Expected Artifacts:
- `src/supply_chain/transparency_verifier.rs`, `tests/security/transparency_inclusion.rs`, `artifacts/10.13/transparency_proof_receipts.json`.

- Machine-readable verification artifact at `artifacts/section_10_13/bd-1z9s/verification_evidence.json` for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary at `artifacts/section_10_13/bd-1z9s/verification_summary.md` linking implementation intent to measured outcomes.
Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for core logic, invariants, and error handling.
- Integration/E2E tests for end-to-end control-flow behavior.
- Detailed structured logs/telemetry with stable codes and trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay fixture or reproducible artifact for failure analysis.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- For "[10.13] Implement transparency-log inclusion proof checks in install/update pipelines.", preserve full capability scope from the plan; no feature compression or silent omission is allowed.
- Verification for "[10.13] Implement transparency-log inclusion proof checks in install/update pipelines." must include capability-specific thresholds and machine-readable pass/fail evidence artifacts.
- Unit tests, integration/E2E scripts, and structured logs for "[10.13] Implement transparency-log inclusion proof checks in install/update pipelines." must support deterministic replay and root-cause triage without hidden context.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[10.13] Implement transparency-log inclusion proof checks in install/update pipelines." is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[10.13] Implement transparency-log inclusion proof checks in install/update pipelines." prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:52.699603053Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T11:43:23.636373487Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T11:43:23.636346347Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-13","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1z9s","depends_on_id":"bd-35q1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:12.928750837Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1zym","title":"[10.14] Implement automatic hardening trigger on guardrail rejection evidence.","description":"## Why This Exists
When a guardrail monitor blocks a policy recommendation (bd-3a3q), it means the system encountered a condition that was dangerous enough to override data-driven advice. This is a strong signal that the system's current hardening level may be insufficient. The automatic hardening trigger responds to guardrail rejection evidence by escalating the hardening state machine (bd-3rya) within a configured latency bound, closing the gap between "we detected a threat" and "we increased our defenses." This creates a feedback loop: guardrail violations trigger hardening, which makes the system more resistant to future violations. The trigger is inspired by FrankenSQLite's automatic journal escalation on corruption detection (9J enhancement map) and supports Section 8.5 Invariant #4 (monotonic safety progression) by ensuring hardening responses are timely and automatic.

## What This Must Do
1. Implement `HardeningAutoTrigger` in `crates/franken-node/src/policy/hardening_auto_trigger.rs` with:
   - `fn on_guardrail_rejection(rejection: &GuardrailRejection, state_machine: &mut HardeningStateMachine) -> TriggerResult` — evaluates the rejection and potentially escalates hardening.
   - `TriggerResult` enum: `Escalated { from: HardeningLevel, to: HardeningLevel, latency_ms: u64 }`, `AlreadyAtMax`, `Suppressed { reason: String }` (e.g., rate limited by clamp).
   - Configurable latency bound: hardening MUST escalate within `max_trigger_latency_ms` of the rejection event (default: 100ms).
2. Make the trigger path idempotent:
   - Multiple rejections at the same level produce the same escalation (not multiple escalations).
   - Idempotency key is derived from `(current_level, rejection.budget_id, epoch_id)`.
3. Include causal evidence pointers in trigger events:
   - `TriggerEvent` struct: `trigger_id`, `rejection_id` (links to the guardrail rejection that caused this), `evidence_entry_id` (links to the evidence ledger entry), `from_level`, `to_level`, `timestamp`.
   - Trigger events are written to both the evidence ledger and the hardening state history.
4. Write integration tests at `tests/integration/hardening_auto_trigger.rs` covering:
   - Guardrail rejection triggers escalation within latency bound.
   - Duplicate rejections at same level are idempotent.
   - Trigger event includes correct causal pointers.
   - `AlreadyAtMax` is returned when at highest level.
5. Write specification at `docs/specs/hardening_trigger_policy.md` documenting trigger conditions, latency requirements, idempotency rules, and causal evidence linking.
6. Produce trigger events artifact at `artifacts/10.14/hardening_trigger_events.jsonl` (JSONL format) recording all trigger events from a test run.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Guardrail rejection triggers hardening within configured latency bound; trigger path is idempotent; trigger events include causal evidence pointer.
- Escalation occurs within `max_trigger_latency_ms` of rejection (verified by test with timer).
- Duplicate rejections at the same level do not cause multiple escalations.
- `TriggerEvent.rejection_id` correctly links to the originating guardrail rejection.
- `TriggerEvent.evidence_entry_id` correctly links to the evidence ledger entry.
- At maximum hardening level, trigger returns `AlreadyAtMax` without error.
- Trigger events artifact is valid JSONL with one event per line.

## Testing & Logging Requirements
- Unit tests: Single rejection triggers escalation; duplicate rejection is idempotent; rejection at max level returns `AlreadyAtMax`; latency measurement within bound; causal pointer correctness; idempotency key derivation.
- Integration tests: Full flow: policy decision -> guardrail rejection -> auto trigger -> state machine escalation -> evidence entry emitted; latency bound verification with wall-clock measurement; multiple rejections from different guardrails at same level.
- Conformance tests: Idempotency across 100 duplicate rejections; causal chain integrity — trigger event's `rejection_id` resolves to a real rejection record; trigger events appear in both evidence ledger and state history.
- Adversarial tests: Rejection with invalid `budget_id`; trigger during ongoing escalation (concurrent safety); rejection with future `epoch_id`; rapid-fire 1000 rejections in 1ms (stress test idempotency).
- Structured logs: `EVD-AUTOTRIG-001` on trigger fired (includes from/to level, latency); `EVD-AUTOTRIG-002` on trigger suppressed (includes reason); `EVD-AUTOTRIG-003` on already-at-max; `EVD-AUTOTRIG-004` on idempotent dedup. All logs include `epoch_id`, `rejection_id`.

## Expected Artifacts
- `crates/franken-node/src/policy/hardening_auto_trigger.rs` — implementation
- `tests/integration/hardening_auto_trigger.rs` — integration tests
- `docs/specs/hardening_trigger_policy.md` — specification
- `artifacts/10.14/hardening_trigger_events.jsonl` — trigger events (JSONL)
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1zym/verification_evidence.json` — machine-readable pass/fail evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1zym/verification_summary.md` — human-readable summary

## Dependencies
- Upstream: bd-3rya (hardening state machine — provides the escalation API), bd-3a3q (guardrail monitors — produces the rejection events that trigger hardening)
- Downstream: bd-3epz (section gate), bd-5rh (10.14 plan gate)","acceptance_criteria":"Guardrail rejection triggers hardening within configured latency bound; trigger path is idempotent; trigger events include causal evidence pointer.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:56.138256697Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T18:48:33.897253922Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T18:48:33.897210110Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-14","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1zym","depends_on_id":"bd-3a3q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T16:23:52.829364638Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1zym","depends_on_id":"bd-3rya","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:14.725796218Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
